title: Should libraries lend out tools, seeds, and instruments — not just books?
summary: Argue whether public libraries should expand their lending programmes to include physical tools, garden seeds, and musical instruments.
topic: Whether public libraries should lend more than books
prompt: Write a 350–500-word essay arguing whether libraries should lend tools, seeds, and instruments — not just books. Give two concrete examples and acknowledge one counter-argument. Use cleft sentences, concession, and hedging.
hintStarters: What a library offers at its best is not a collection of objects but access to…
Granted, the case for keeping libraries focused on books is not without…
It is precisely because so many households cannot afford a power drill that…
The question is not whether libraries can lend tools, but whether…

thư viện công cụ

mở rộng phạm vi nhiệm vụ

làm căng thẳng nguồn lực

thách thức về mặt hậu cần

lợi ích công cộng / hàng hóa công
gánh nặng bảo trì
bất bình đẳng thu nhập
mở cửa cho tất cả mọi người
nhận trách nhiệm về / làm chủ

sampleText:
The modern library was never simply a warehouse of books. It was, from its earliest days, a bet on the idea that access to knowledge should not be determined by income. What a library offers at its best is not a collection of objects but a democratised gateway to capability — and there is no obvious reason why that gateway should stop at the printed page.

The tool-lending library at the Berkeley Public Library in California, in operation since 1979, demonstrates how seamlessly this principle extends to physical objects. Residents can borrow power drills, soil testers, and ladders that most households would use only a handful of times a year. The library does not ask whether a borrower can afford to own these tools; it simply makes them available. A comparable logic underlies the seed libraries now operating through the Edinburgh Central Library network, where local growers can borrow packets of heritage vegetable varieties, cultivate them through the season, and return seeds at harvest. Both programmes rest on the same conviction: that a civic institution exists to expand what its community can do, not merely what it can read.

Granted, the objection that such programmes dilute a library's core mission is not entirely without force. Libraries operate under tight budgets, and a drill borrowed by ten people is subject to wear in a way that a book borrowed by ten people is not. The maintenance burden is real, and the risk of stretching limited staff time across an ever-wider catalogue is a legitimate concern that administrators are right to weigh carefully.

Even so, the evidence from existing programmes suggests that logistical challenges are manageable rather than prohibitive. Borrowing periods tend to be shorter for tools than for books, reducing stock pressure, and the communities that benefit most — households facing income inequality, urban gardeners without access to specialist equipment — are often those a library is formally committed to serving. What is being expanded here is not a distraction from the public good but an extension of it.

Libraries that lend only books are, it might be argued, already choosing a particular kind of object. The question is not whether a library should lend things but which things best serve its community at a given moment. Tools, seeds, and instruments are, in that light, entirely consistent with what libraries have always been.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to redefine the library's purpose
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a library offers at its best is not a collection of objects but a democratised gateway to capability' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để tái định nghĩa đối tượng đang được thảo luận, trì hoãn kết luận quan trọng nhất đến cuối câu. Cấu trúc 'not X but Y' nhấn mạnh sự tương phản một cách rõ ràng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a library offers at its best is not a collection of objects but a democratised gateway to capability' uses a what-cleft to redefine the subject under discussion, delaying the key conclusion until the end of the clause. The 'not X but Y' structure sharpens the contrast.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Hedging with 'it might be argued' and 'tend to'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Libraries that lend only books are, it might be argued, already choosing a particular kind of object' và 'Borrowing periods tend to be shorter' dùng hedging để tránh tuyệt đối hóa. 'It might be argued' chèn vào giữa câu như một mệnh đề chèn, tạo khoảng cách giữa tác giả và lập luận; 'tend to' thay thế 'always' để tránh khẳng định quá mức.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Libraries that lend only books are, it might be argued, already choosing a particular kind of object' and 'Borrowing periods tend to be shorter' both hedge their claims. 'It might be argued', inserted as a parenthetical, distances the writer from the assertion; 'tend to' replaces 'always' to avoid over-claiming.

sampleTranslationVi:
Thư viện hiện đại chưa bao giờ chỉ đơn giản là một kho sách. Ngay từ những ngày đầu, nó là một cuộc đặt cược vào ý tưởng rằng quyền tiếp cận kiến thức không nên bị quyết định bởi thu nhập. Điều mà một thư viện cung cấp tốt nhất không phải là một bộ sưu tập đồ vật mà là một cổng vào được dân chủ hóa cho năng lực — và không có lý do rõ ràng nào tại sao cổng đó lại dừng lại ở trang in.

Thư viện công cụ tại Thư viện Công cộng Berkeley ở California, hoạt động từ năm 1979, cho thấy nguyên tắc này mở rộng đến các vật thể vật lý một cách liền mạch như thế nào. Cư dân có thể mượn máy khoan điện, thiết bị kiểm tra đất và thang, những thứ mà hầu hết các hộ gia đình chỉ dùng vài lần một năm. Thư viện không hỏi liệu người mượn có đủ khả năng sở hữu những công cụ này hay không; nó đơn giản là cung cấp sẵn sàng. Một logic tương tự nằm bên dưới các thư viện hạt giống hiện đang hoạt động thông qua mạng lưới Thư viện Trung tâm Edinburgh, nơi người trồng trọt địa phương có thể mượn các gói hạt giống rau truyền thống, trồng chúng suốt mùa vụ và trả lại hạt giống vào mùa thu hoạch. Cả hai chương trình đều dựa trên cùng một niềm tin: rằng một tổ chức dân sự tồn tại để mở rộng những gì cộng đồng của nó có thể làm, không chỉ những gì nó có thể đọc.

Công nhận, phản đối rằng các chương trình như vậy làm loãng sứ mệnh cốt lõi của thư viện không hoàn toàn thiếu căn cứ. Thư viện hoạt động trong ngân sách eo hẹp, và một cái khoan được mượn bởi mười người sẽ bị mòn theo cách mà một cuốn sách được mượn bởi mười người thì không. Gánh nặng bảo trì là có thật, và nguy cơ kéo giãn thời gian nhân viên hạn chế trên một danh mục ngày càng rộng hơn là mối lo ngại chính đáng mà các quản trị viên có lý khi cân nhắc cẩn thận.

Dẫu vậy, bằng chứng từ các chương trình hiện có cho thấy những thách thức hậu cần có thể quản lý được chứ không phải là cấm đoán. Thời gian mượn có xu hướng ngắn hơn đối với dụng cụ so với sách, giảm áp lực tồn kho, và những cộng đồng được hưởng lợi nhiều nhất — các hộ gia đình đối mặt với bất bình đẳng thu nhập, những người làm vườn đô thị không có quyền tiếp cận thiết bị chuyên dụng — thường là những đối tượng mà thư viện được cam kết chính thức phục vụ. Những gì đang được mở rộng ở đây không phải là sự xao nhãng khỏi lợi ích công mà là sự mở rộng của nó.

Các thư viện chỉ cho mượn sách, có thể lập luận rằng, đã đang lựa chọn một loại đồ vật cụ thể. Câu hỏi không phải là liệu thư viện có nên cho mượn đồ vật hay không mà là đồ vật nào phục vụ tốt nhất cho cộng đồng của nó tại một thời điểm nhất định. Dụng cụ, hạt giống và nhạc cụ, xét theo quan điểm đó, hoàn toàn nhất quán với những gì thư viện luôn luôn là.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to reframe the library's purpose?
options: What a library offers at its best is not a collection of objects but a democratised gateway to capability.
It is what libraries offer that is best considered a gateway to capability.
Libraries offer the best collection of objects and gateways to capability.
A library is what offers capability through a democratised collection.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What a library offers … is …' is the what-cleft structure, delaying the key contrast ('not X but Y') to the end for rhetorical emphasis.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the crucial contrast placed at the very end.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence introduces a concession most naturally for B2 argumentative writing?
options: On the other hand libraries are bad.
Granted, the objection that such programmes dilute a library's core mission is not entirely without force.
Although libraries are good some people disagree so that is also a point.
Of course there are problems with libraries that everybody already knows about.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' opens the concession cleanly, and 'not entirely without force' is precise hedging that avoids dismissing the objection outright.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses the formal concession opener and hedged language together?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses hedging to avoid over-claiming?
options: Borrowing periods are always shorter for tools than for books.
Borrowing periods tend to be shorter for tools than for books, reducing stock pressure.
Borrowing periods must definitely be shorter for tools, eliminating stock pressure absolutely.
Tools are borrowed for shorter periods, which proves stock pressure is eliminated.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tend to be' hedges the claim by expressing a general pattern rather than an absolute rule, appropriate for B2 academic argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrasing softens the claim without making it sound uncertain or evasive?

options: To restrict access to elected officials only
To make access available to everyone regardless of income or status
To vote on which items the library should stock
To charge a small fee to make services self-sustaining
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Democratise' means to make something available to all people, removing barriers linked to wealth or privilege.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'democracy' means and apply it to access.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the passive to foreground the object being acted upon?
options: The library makes tools available to residents who visit.
Tools are made available by the library regardless of whether a borrower can afford to own them.
Libraries making tools available is what residents can visit for.
Residents make tools available at the library when they visit.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tools are made available' places 'tools' in subject position, emphasising what is offered rather than who offers it — appropriate for an institutional argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option puts 'tools' (not 'library' or 'residents') at the start of the clause?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best describes the Edinburgh seed library programmes?
options: Shops where gardeners buy seeds at a discount
Programmes where borrowers take seeds, grow crops, and return seeds at harvest
Online databases of plant information for urban farmers
Government schemes that distribute free seeds by post
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay describes seed libraries as places to 'borrow packets of heritage vegetable varieties, cultivate them through the season, and return seeds at harvest.'
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the Edinburgh Central Library description in the sample text.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to dilute a library's core mission' mean in this context?
options: To add water to library facilities
To weaken the library's main purpose by spreading resources too thinly
To translate library catalogues into other languages
To reduce the opening hours of the library
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Dilute' in its figurative sense means to weaken by mixing in elements that do not belong — here applied to the library's primary educational function.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'dilute' as a liquid metaphor: what happens when you add something that weakens the concentration?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'it might be argued' correctly as a hedging parenthetical?
options: Libraries that lend only books are, it might be argued, already choosing a particular kind of object.
It might be argued libraries lend only books that are choosing a particular object.
Libraries it might be argued lend only choosing a particular object of books.
It might be argued that libraries only lend choosing particular objects which are books.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It might be argued' is inserted as a parenthetical clause between commas, softening the main claim without removing it from the sentence.
mcQuestions.hint: A parenthetical clause is surrounded by commas and can be removed without breaking the grammar of the main sentence.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after the concession?
options: In conclusion, the problems are real.
Even so, the evidence from existing programmes suggests that logistical challenges are manageable.
On the contrary, the problems do not exist at all.
However this is not a problem for the library.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' acknowledges the concession in the previous paragraph and pivots back to the main argument with a counter-evidence move.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both acknowledges what was granted and introduces evidence that reduces its force?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is a 'maintenance burden' in the context of tool lending?
options: The cost of publishing library catalogues
The ongoing work and cost of repairing and upkeeping tools that are frequently used
The administrative process of registering new borrowers
The weight of heavy tools that need to be transported
mcQuestions.explanation: Unlike books, tools wear with use and require checking, repairing, or replacing — this ongoing operational cost is the 'maintenance burden'.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what happens to a drill after fifty people borrow it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word means 'making something available to all people regardless of wealth'?
options: prohibitive
underutilised
democratised
sustained
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Democratised' describes a process or system where access is opened to all, removing economic or social barriers.
mcQuestions.hint: Which word connects to the root meaning of 'democracy' — rule by, and for, the people?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option maintains formal academic register throughout?
options: Tool libraries are super cool and everybody loves them so libraries should just do this.
The evidence from existing programmes suggests that logistical challenges are manageable rather than prohibitive.
Tool lending is obviously a great idea and no one really thinks it's that hard.
Libraries definitely should lend tools and stuff because poor people really need drills and things.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses formal vocabulary ('logistical challenges', 'manageable', 'prohibitive'), hedged phrasing ('suggests'), and a parallel 'X rather than Y' structure.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid intensifiers like 'super', vague words like 'stuff', and informal contractions.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'heritage variety' mean in the context of seed libraries?
options: Seeds imported from other countries
A plant variety commercially modified for mass production
A traditional plant variety preserved through generations, not modified by industrial breeding
A seed that produces only one harvest per year
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Heritage varieties' are old, non-hybrid, open-pollinated plant types often associated with traditional farming and biodiversity conservation.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'heritage' as meaning passed down through generations, unchanged by commercial pressure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a participle clause correctly to express a result?
options: Borrowing periods tend to be shorter for tools than for books, reducing stock pressure.
Reducing borrowing periods for tools than for books, stock pressure tends shorter.
Stock pressure borrowing periods for tools shorter than books tends to reducing.
Tools than for books borrowing periods tend to reducing stock pressure shorter.
mcQuestions.explanation: A present participle clause ('reducing stock pressure') attached after the comma logically expresses the result of the shorter borrowing periods stated in the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: The participial phrase at the end should express the result or consequence of the main clause.

options: A private company that provides services to the government
An organisation established by and for the public community, typically funded publicly
A religious organisation that serves local communities
A school or university offering civic education
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Civic' relates to the public life of a community; a 'civic institution' is one that belongs to and serves all members of that community.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'civic' as connected to citizenship and public life, not private or religious life.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what is the strongest argument for expanding library lending?
options: Tools and seeds generate more borrowing income than books.
Most households already own the tools they need.
Expanding lending to tools and seeds serves the same communities a library is already committed to helping.
Seed libraries are cheaper to run than book libraries.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that the communities most benefiting — those facing income inequality — are 'those a library is formally committed to serving.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final sentence of the fourth paragraph.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If you could design a tool-lending or seed-lending programme for a library in your city, what three items would you prioritise and why? Who specifically would benefit most, and how would you handle wear and maintenance?
